params.text:
Making the environment safer

Small changes to a person’s surroundings can prevent many falls. Look at where someone walks, stands and moves most, and make those places as safe as you can.

  • Handrails and grab rails: secure rails on stairs, and grab rails by the toilet, bath and bed, give people something firm to hold. They must be fitted properly by a competent person.
  • Flooring: non-slip flooring and firmly fixed, well-fitting floor coverings reduce slips; remove or tape down loose rugs and mats.
  • Lighting: good, even lighting, including a night light on the route to the toilet, helps people see hazards.
  • Clear routes: keep walkways free of clutter, trailing cables and spills.

Equipment that supports safe movement

The right equipment helps a person move confidently and independently.

  • Walking aids: sticks, frames and rollators must be the correct height and in good repair, and kept within the person’s reach.
  • Footwear: well-fitting, supportive shoes or slippers with a firm, non-slip sole are far safer than loose slippers or socks.
  • Sensor mats and alarms: pressure mats or falls sensors can alert you when someone at high risk is getting up, so you can offer help in time.
  • Hip protectors: padded garments that cushion the hip may be suggested for some people at high risk of fracture, as part of a wider plan.

Equipment should always be recommended through assessment — often by an occupational therapist or physiotherapist — and you should only ever use it as you have been trained and as set out in the person’s care plan.

params.question: Sensor or pressure mats are used so that staff can restrain a person and stop them getting up on their own.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. This is false. Sensor mats do not restrain anyone; they alert staff so support can be offered promptly, while respecting the person’s freedom to move.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this is false. Sensor mats are not a means of restraint. They simply alert staff that someone at high risk is getting up, so help can be offered in time while the person keeps their freedom to move.
